results.query: Summarize diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment considerations for intra-abdominal laparoscopic surgical fire exceedingly rare.
results.rewritten_query: Summarize diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment considerations for intra-abdominal laparoscopic surgical fire exceedingly rare.

results.text:
It was noteworthy that 44% of these surgeons and presumably their
accompanying anesthesia provider had already experienced at least one OR
fire, with on average only 17.7 years in practice, and 12.5% of the surgeons
had already experienced more than one fire.111 Another survey of 258
oculoplastic surgeons found that 32% of surgeons had experienced at least
one fire in the OR and 10 as many as three fires. Eyelid surgeries were the
most common (90%) eye surgery associated with fire. Based on these
numbers, this study estimated there would be 14 fires each year related to
oculoplastic surgery alone.112
Abdominal Surgery/Colonoscopy Fires
During open abdominal procedures and colonoscopy, even room air provides
adequate oxidizer for flammable intestinal gases (e.g., hydrogen, methane) to
burn.114–116 Similar conditions exist in open abdominal surgery with either a
bowel perforation and release of gas into the peritoneal cavity or during
entry into a viscus with a cautery.117 In modern practice, carbon dioxide is
increasingly used as the insufflating gas during gastrointestinal endoscopy
because it is more comfortable for patients and recognized as safer than air
in the event of a gas embolus. It has the incidental benefit of reducing fire
risk.118
Intrabdominal 
surgical 
procedures 
are 
increasingly 
performed
laparoscopically. Because the insufflation gas is 100% carbon dioxide,
which is fire quenching, the fire risk from the abdominal gases is low.
However, if oxygen is accidentally used for insufflation, fire can and has
occured.119
Thoracic Surgery Fires
An operation using cautery in the chest may also result in an in-the-patient
fire.120,121 These fires generally follow some violation of the lung
parenchyma, resulting in an enriched oxygen environment within the thorax or
mediastinum accompanied by cautery and dry lap sponges.
The typically higher FiO2 used in thoracic and cardiac surgery patients
increases the risk when there is an opening in the lung. This is also true

bleeding, while managing possible hemodynamic instability due to
hemorrhagic shock. Intraoperative testing and interpretation of hematocrit as
an assessment of acute blood loss should be performed cautiously, given the
confounding effects of preoperative hematocrit collection, active bleeding,
and intravascular dilution from crystalloid infusion. Transfusion triggers
should be made on an individualized basis, taking into account estimated
blood loss as well as the patient’s hemodynamics and coexisting diseases.
Despite these considerations, in general, the risk of bleeding complications
during laparoscopic procedures is rare and routine preoperative type and
screen testing is not necessary.102,103
Gastrointestinal structures can be injured during both abdominal entry
and the intra-abdominal portion of the procedure.95 Bowel injuries are
infrequent but are a major cause of morbidity and mortality. Because many
intestinal injuries go unrecognized, when they do occur, the risk of
postoperative intra-abdominal sepsis is high, making it a common cause of
death related to laparoscopy. Deflation of the stomach with an orogastric
tube should be routinely performed prior to the start of surgery to minimize
the risk of gastric injury during left upper quadrant trocar insertion. Bladder
perforation and ureter ligation or transection are also possible during
secondary trocar placement or in the context of dissection during
laparoscopic hysterectomy. Low urine output, hematuria, and more rarely,
pneumaturia may be signs of urologic injury during surgery. If suspected, the
presence of an injury can be confirmed and possibly repaired via cystoscopy.
Postoperative lower abdominal discomfort, unexpected gross hematuria, or
rapidly rising serum creatinine should trigger immediate suspicion of occult
injury.
Cardiopulmonary
12  Acute cardiovascular complications associated with laparoscopy
include hypertension, hypotension, dysrhythmias, and rarely, cardiac arrest.
Hemodynamic and pulmonary changes are mediated by CO2 insufflation,
increased IAP, and hypercarbia. Initial insufflation at an IAP of 5 mmHg in
normovolemic patients leads to an increase in venous return due to
displacement of blood from the splanchnic vasculature, a rise in cardiac
output, and hypertension. Catecholamine release further aggravates

the surgical field. Then, and consistent with patient needs, the oxygen
concentration should be limited to 30% or less; otherwise, it is advised to
seal the airway.62
In addition, for high–fire-risk procedures, moistened sponges should be
used because they are less flammable, a basin or bottle of sterile irrigating
fluid should be readily available, and ignition sources should be kept away
from the drapes. When a high fire risk is identified during the timeout, all
parties, who each control a limb of the fire triangle, should acknowledge
their awareness and preemptive plan. This communication is essential. The
fire risk for a procedure may increase or decrease as the procedure evolves.
OR Fires and Lessons from Closed Claims
and Review Findings
The most recent closed claims analysis of OR fires was published in 2013
and includes cases dating to 1985.70 The findings remain instructive because
most of the underlying hazards still exist. In that analysis of 103 fires,
electrosurgery was the heat/ignition source in 90% of cases. Most fires
(81%) occurred during monitored anesthesia care or regional anesthesia
care. A recurring theme was that supplemental oxygen was administered via
an open system (nasal cannula or face mask). Of the fires in the closed claims
analysis, 85% occurred during head, neck, or upper chest procedures.
Significantly, the authors observed that oxygen was often administered at
flow rates and/or concentrations higher than what were likely needed to
maintain an acceptable oxygen saturation. For fires occurring under general
anesthesia, tracheostomy and tonsillectomy were the two most common
operations.70 The recurring etiology appears to be electrosurgery in the
presence of an elevated oxidizer concentration at the operative site, that is,
an oxygen ± nitrous oxide concentration >30% from a leak into the operative
site, for example, an uncuffed tube. In a separate review of fires associated
with tonsillectomy, an uncuffed tube and oxygen concentration >30% were
noted in 35 of 36 reported cases.71,72 A series of images from a manikin
simulation set up to duplicate a case where a fire occurred during a burr hole
placement with supplemental oxygen by face mask and monopolar
electrosurgery provide a vivid demonstration of the speed and intensity of the

results.query: How does respiratory depressant effects propofol dose dependent affect perioperative or critical-care decision making?
results.rewritten_query: How does respiratory depressant effects propofol dose dependent affect perioperative or critical-care decision making?

results.text:
The respiratory depressant effects of propofol are also dose dependent.
Apnea is relatively common with a higher induction dose, while a typical
maintenance dose of propofol results in diminished tidal volumes and
increased respiratory rate. There is also a blunted response to hypoxia that
may be a direct effect on chemoreceptors, as well as a decreased respiratory
response to hypercarbia. Propofol is a potent bronchodilator, primarily
because of its direct effects on intracellular calcium homeostasis.
Clinical Uses
4  The rapid and smooth induction and emergence from anesthesia helped
transform propofol into an intravenous sedative–hypnotic that is a viable
alternative to standard inhalational agents and other intravenous drugs. The
induction dose in a healthy adult is approximately 1 to 2.5 mg/kg, and loss of
consciousness is commonly achieved at corresponding blood concentration
close to 3 mcg/kg. Induction dose requirement variability is tremendous
among patients with different characteristics and comorbidities. Elderly
patients typically have prolonged effects and increased sensitivity to
propofol because of decreased cardiac output and clearance.25 On the
opposite end of the spectrum, children typically have a larger than average
volume of distribution and quicker clearance, resulting in increased propofol
requirement on a per-kilogram basis.26 Morbidly obese patients should have
lean body weight used when calculating propofol dosing.27 Patients with
chronic alcohol abuse, as expected, have an increased induction dose
requirement. An exaggerated hemodynamic response is likely after induction
of propofol in patients with cardiovascular disease. Thus, determination of
the appropriate propofol induction dose requires careful assessment of
premedication administration, patient history, and comorbidity.
Maintenance of general anesthesia with propofol can commonly be
achieved with infusions rate between 100 to 200 mcg/kg/min. TIVA with
propofol alone—or together with opioids as part of a balanced anesthetic—
has been utilized successfully for all types of surgery. One of the major
benefits is prevention of postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV),
although the antiemetic properties of propofol can be replicated with
subhypnotic concentrations. Maintenance infusions as low as 10 mcg/kg/min

Figure 18-17 Stress hormone responses to a rapid increase in anesthetic
concentration, from 4% to 12% inspired. Volunteers given desflurane showed a larger
increase in plasma epinephrine and norepinephrine concentrations than when given isoflurane.
Data are mean ± SE. A, awake value; B, value after 32 minutes of 0.55 minimum alveolar
concentration; time represents minutes after the first breath of increased anesthetic
concentration. (Adapted with permission from Weiskopf RB, Moore MA, Eger EI II, et al. Rapid
increase in desflurane concentration is associated with greater transient cardiovascular
stimulation than with rapid increase in isoflurane concentration in humans. Anesthesiology.
1994;80:1035.)
The Pulmonary System
General Ventilatory Effects
11  All volatile anesthetics decrease tidal volume and increase respiratory
rate such that the decrease in minute ventilation at low inspired
concentrations is relatively minor (Fig. 18-18). The ventilatory depressant
effects of volatile anesthetics at higher concentrations are dose dependent,
with higher concentrations of volatile anesthetics resulting in greater
decreases in tidal volume and greater increases in respiratory rate. Isoflurane
is an exception in that it does not increase respiratory rate above 1 MAC.
The net effect of a gradual decrease in minute ventilation is an associated
increase in resting PaCO2. The respiratory depression of volatile anesthetics
can be partially antagonized by the “pain” of surgical stimulation. Nitrous
oxide increases respiratory rate as much or more than the volatile
anesthetics. When N2O is added to inhaled volatile anesthetics PaCO2

Figure 18-19 All inhaled anesthetics produce similar dose-dependent decreases in the
ventilatory response to carbon dioxide (CO2). N2O, nitrous oxide. (Adapted from Eger EI 2nd.
Desflurane. Anesth Rev. 1993;20:87.)
Inhaled anesthetics, including N2O, also produce a dose-dependent
attenuation of the ventilatory response to hypoxia. This action appears to
depend on the peripheral chemoreceptors. In fact, even subanesthetic
concentrations of volatile anesthetics (0.1 MAC) elicit anywhere from a 25%
to 75% depression of the ventilatory drive to hypoxia (Fig. 18-20). The
extreme sensitivity of the volatile anesthetics to inhibit ventilatory responses
to hypoxia has important clinical implications, especially in patients who
depend on hypoxic drive to set their level of ventilation, such as those with

Figure 18-20 Influence of 0.1 minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) of four volatile anesthetic
agents on the ventilatory response to a step decrease in end-tidal oxygen concentration.
Values are mean ± SD. Subanesthetic concentrations of the volatile anesthetics, except
desflurane and sevoflurane, profoundly depress the response to hypoxia. (Adapted with
permission from Sarton E, Dahan A, Teppema L, et al. Acute pain and central nervous system
arousal do not restore impaired hypoxic ventilatory responses during sevoflurane sedation.
Anesthesiology. 1996;85:295.)
Airway smooth muscle extends as far distally as the terminal bronchioles
and is under the influence of both parasympathetic and sympathetic nerves.
The volatile anesthetics relax airway smooth muscle primarily by directly
reducing smooth muscle tone and indirectly by inhibiting the reflex neural
pathways.99 Direct effects of the volatile anesthetics partially depend on an
intact bronchial epithelium, suggesting that epithelial damage or inflammation
secondary to asthma or a respiratory virus may lessen their bronchodilating
effect.100 In animal models studying fourth-order bronchi, desflurane and
sevoflurane were better bronchodilators than isoflurane.100 The dilation was
epithelium dependent and mediated in part by a cyclooxygenase product and
nitric oxide. In humans, early administration of desflurane after tracheal
intubation and high concentrations (1.5 MAC) at steady state will transiently
increase airway resistance (Fig. 18-21).101,102 This may be attributed to a
direct effect on bronchial smooth muscle from the pungency of desflurane.

Figure 18-18 Comparison of mean changes in resting PaCO2, tidal volume, respiratory rate,
and minute ventilation in patients anesthetized with isoflurane, sevoflurane, desflurane, or
N2O(N). Anesthetic-induced tachypnea compensates in part for the ventilatory depression
caused by all volatile anesthetics (decrease in minute ventilation and tidal volume, and
concomitant increase in PaCO2). Desflurane results in the greatest increase in PaCO2 with
corresponding reductions in tidal volume and minute ventilation. Isoflurane, like all other inhaled
agents, increases respiratory rate, but does not result in dose-dependent tachypnea. (Adapted
from Anesthesiology. 1991;74:484, Anesthesiology. 1971;35:26, Anesth Analg. 1987;66:241,
Anesth Analg. 1978;57:610.)

needed to determine if this translates to a decrease in postoperative
pulmonary complications and ultimately better surgical outcomes.
Various anesthetic regimens have been studied to determine if particular
amnestic agents, delivery approaches and pain management strategies change
postoperative delirium risk in older patients. Unfortunately, these efforts
have yielded no agreed-upon superior approach for neuroprotection.38,40
Many postoperative nausea and vomiting prophylaxis strategies make use of
Beers Criteria medications that may present an uneven balance of risks and
benefits in older patients: prophylactic steroids, transdermal scopolamine,
promethazine, and prochlorperazine all have cognitive impairment risks.
The utility of electroencephalography (EEG) for directed management of
the anesthetic plan to lower postoperative complications remains an area of
non-consensus. The ENGAGES randomized clinical trial involving 1,232
patients aged 60 years and older undergoing major surgery with volatile
anesthetics, and compared postoperative outcomes and delirium rate between
a BIS-guided anesthetic management group and a “usual care” group. The
BIS-guided group had about a 0.1 MAC reduction. The 30-day mortality rate
was lower in the BIS-guided group (0.65% vs. 3.07%) although no other
postoperative 
comparisons 
showed 
relevant 
differences, 
including
hypotension, PONV, delirium and serious complications.70 The BALANCED
trial was adequately powered to directly compare lighter (BIS target 50) to
deeper (BIS target 35) anesthetic depths with more than 3,300 patients in
each group, and more than double the reduction in volatile agent MAC was
achieved between study groups compared to the ENGAGES trial. However,
there was no benefit in either BIS group for postoperative mortality at 1 year,
and no significant differences in serious adverse events after surgery such as
infection and cardiac problems.71 Through a subanalysis, which much be
interpreted with caution, 515 patients from the BALANCED trial were
evaluated for postoperative delirium and a significant reduction in delirium
incidence was reported in the BIS 50 group (19%) compared to the BIS 35
group (28%, odds ratio 0.58 [95% confidence interval: 0.38–0.88, P < .01]).
Likewise, cognitive function at 1-year postsurgery was significantly better in
the lighter anesthesia group based upon the abbreviated mental test score
(AMTS).72 The SHARP randomized clinical trial of 217 elderly patients
undergoing lumbar spinal fusion directly compared spinal anesthesia with
targeted BIS-sedation60–70 versus unguided general anesthesia. Researchers

Figure 31-5 As the dose of neuromuscular blocking agent (intraoperative dose of atracurium,
cisatracurium, rocuronium, or vecuronium) increases, the likelihood of respiratory
complications increases. (Adapted with permission from McLean DJ, Diaz-Gil D, Farhan HN,
Ladha KS, Kurth T, Eikermann M. Dose-dependent association between intermediate-acting
neuromuscular-blocking agents and postoperative respiratory complications. Anesthesiology.
2015;122:1201–1213.)
Management of Postanesthesia Care
Managing common problems in the PACU quickly and effectively is as
important as appropriate patient selection and choice of anesthetic technique
if the patient is to return home on the day of surgery. The three most common
reasons for delay in patient discharge from the PACU are drowsiness, nausea

and more a cardiac mini-ICU. The smart PACU medical director and hospital
administrator will see that with targeted resources, patients may well be
safely cared for in a more cost-effective manner with quicker throughput by
using a PACU approach.
Postoperative Pulmonary Dysfunction
Mechanical, hemodynamic, and pharmacologic factors related to surgery and
anesthesia impair ventilation, oxygenation, and airway maintenance.37 Heavy
smoking, obesity, sleep apnea, severe asthma, and COPD increase the risk of
postoperative ventilatory events.38 Preoperative pulmonary function testing
has limited predictive value for postoperative complications,39 perhaps with
the exception of postoperative bronchospasm in smokers.40
Inadequate Postoperative Ventilation
In PACU patients, mild respiratory acidemia due to decreased minute
ventilation is expected; thus elevated PaCO2 does not necessarily indicate
inadequate postoperative ventilation. Inadequate ventilation should be
suspected when (1) respiratory acidemia occurs coincident with tachypnea,
anxiety, dyspnea, labored ventilation, or increased sympathetic nervous
system activity; (2) hypercarbia reduces the arterial pH below 7.30; or (3)
PaCO2 progressively increases with a progressive decrease in arterial pH.
Inadequate Respiratory Drive
8  During early recovery from anesthesia, residual effects of intravenous
and inhalation anesthetics blunt the ventilatory responses to both hypercarbia
and hypoxemia. Sedatives augment depression from opioids or anesthetics.
Hypoventilation and hypercarbia can evolve insidiously during transfer
and admission to the PACU. Although effects of intraoperative medications
are usually waning, the peak depressant effect of an intravenous opioid given
just before transfer occurs in the PACU. Coincident depression of medullary
centers that regulate the sympathetic nervous system can blunt signs of
acidemia or hypoxemia such as hypertension, tachycardia, and agitation,

results.query: Give a structured review of perner haase guttormsen hydroxyethyl starch versus with practical bedside implications.
results.rewritten_query: Give a structured review of perner haase guttormsen hydroxyethyl starch versus with practical bedside implications.

results.text:
Notably, colloid fluids come suspended in a carrier solution that has
crystalloid solutes of varying tonicity. Some concentrated colloid-containing
solutions can be isotonic or hypertonic and exert sufficient oncotic pressure
to completely stay intravascularly or to even translocate substantial volumes
of IFV into the PV, thereby expanding PV by a volume greater that infused
volume. Minimizing IFV expansion offers some advantages: lower volume
fluid administration, less peripheral and pulmonary edema, and reduced
cardiopulmonary consequences of later fluid mobilization (Table 16-12).
Despite these theoretical advantages of colloids, however, exhaustive
research failed to demonstrate the superiority of either colloid or crystalloid
fluids for intraoperative or postoperative use. The lack of evidence and the
higher cost of colloid, especially albumin, should give the clinician pause
when considering its use.
A 2018 Cochrane66 review of 69 studies (65 RCTs, 4 quasiRCTs) with
30,020 participants examined effects of crystalloid versus colloid
resuscitation in critically ill patients in the hospital and emergency out-of-
hospital setting. Twentyeight studies investigated starch solutions, 20
dextrans, 7 gelatins, and 22 albumin or fresh frozen plasma (FFP). The
authors concluded that colloid versus crystalloid probably makes little or no
difference to mortality; however, starches probably slightly increase the need
for blood transfusion and RRT. Another Cochrane67 review from 2012 of 86
trials, with a total of 5,484 critically ill and surgical patients requiring
resuscitation, examined the outcomes associated with different types of
colloid (albumin, plasma, HES, dextran, and gelatin). The authors concluded
that was no detectable differences in mortality between colloids, although
confidence intervals were wide. Notably, there were only reports of adverse
events related to gelatin and HES. Individual studies subsequent to the above
meta-analysis point to more effective volume expansion, lower overall fluid
administration, and less vasopressor requirements,68,69 although there are
studies show conflicting results regarding mortality and morbidity.70,71
5  The use of albumin in particular has persisted despite the lack of
conclusive evidence of efficacy.72 In patients with sepsis or septic shock, the
Early Albumin Resuscitation during Septic Shock (EARSS) study73 and the
Albumin Italian Outcome Sepsis (ALBIOS) trial74 failed to find any
difference in their primary outcomes of 28-day mortality. However, in

diabetes. Hourly measurements are reasonable in high-risk patients,
especially those receiving continuous insulin through either an insulin pump
or infusion.
The standard glucose dosage for an adult patient is 5 to 10 g/h (100 to
200 mL of 5% dextrose solution hourly). Intraoperative administration of
glucose should be guided by the patient’s glucose level. Routine
administration 
of 
additional 
glucose-containing 
IV 
fluids 
is 
not
recommended. It is best to separately record dextrose administration and
fluids administered.
Monitoring of the patient who arrives in the operating room with a
significant metabolic impairment such as DKA is similar to management in
the medical ICU, and should include hourly determinations of blood glucose,
arterial pH, electrolytes, and fluid balance. Frequent reassessments with
medical consultation as necessary guide the use of fluids and electrolytes,
especially potassium, insulin, phosphate, and glucose.
Another area of monitoring that is extremely important in the diabetic
patient is positioning on the operating table. Injuries to the limbs or nerves
are more likely in the patient who arrives in the operating room already
compromised by diabetic peripheral vascular disease or neuropathy. The
peripheral nerves may already be partly ischemic and therefore particularly
vulnerable to pressure or stretch injuries.
Hyperglycemia and Perioperative Outcomes
Prior to 2001, little attention was paid to the control of hyperglycemia in the
perioperative period or in the acute phase of critical illness managed in the
ICU. Permissive or stress-induced hyperglycemia was generally accepted as
the norm. Stress-induced hyperglycemia is defined as a transient response to
the stress of an acute injury or illness.57 Observational studies have reported
a significant prevalence of hyperglycemia in hospitalized patients. Seventy
percent of diabetic patients with acute coronary syndrome and 80% of
cardiac surgery patients in the perioperative period may develop
hyperglycemia.67 Hyperglycemia in a hospital setting is defined as any blood
glucose higher than 140 mg/dL.85 Hyperglycemia significantly impairs
chemotaxis, phagocytosis, generation of reactive oxygen species, and

Saline versus Albumin for Fluid Resuscitation in Patients with Traumatic
Brain Injury (SAFE) Trial, 2-year follow-up of 460 patients with TBI
(Glasgow Coma Scale score ≤13) demonstrated a nearly twofold increased
risk of death in patients receiving colloid.89 Secondary analysis of the SAFE
trial data suggested that patients receiving 4% albumin had a higher
incidence of refractory intracranial hypertension, which may be the
mechanism of increased mortality.90 However, Van Aken et al.91 pointed out
that the 4% albumin solution used in the SAFE trial was suspended in a
hypo-osmolar carrier solution, so it is still debated whether the albumin, the
reduced osmolarity, or perhaps both were at fault (
 NICV 16-2).
Notably, in the experimental animal studies above, 5% albumin was
suspended in a slightly hyperosmolar solution in contrast to the hypo-osmolar
4% solution used in the SAFE clinical trial.91 Although the role of colloid in
resuscitation of brain-injured patients remains unclear, it is appropriate to
avoid large volumes of hypo-osmotic or hypo-oncotic fluids.92
Buffered versus Nonbuffered Crystalloid
6  There has been much interest recently in the differences in effect between
infusions of buffered and nonbuffered crystalloid solutions. Individual
studies reported metabolic derangements, particularly hyperchloremic
metabolic acidosis, and more importantly AKI.93,94 However, data collected
prior to these studies included in a 2017 Cochrane95 review of 18
randomized controlled trials with a total of 1,096 patients examining the
effects on buffered versus nonbuffered crystalloid IV fluids on adult surgical
patients. They found insufficient evidence on the effects of fluid therapies on
mortality and postoperative organ dysfunction, or renal insufficiency. There
were differences in postoperative pH of at least 0.05 units (95% CI 0.04–
0.07; I² = 61%), although this difference resolved by postoperative day 1.
They also observed higher postoperative serum chloride with nonbuffered
fluids (MD 6.77 mmol/L, 95% CI 3.38–10.17) which persisted until
postoperative day 1. The clinical significance of any of these positive
findings is questionable. Similarly, a 2016 Cochrane96 review examined the
effects of normal saline versus low chloride solution for adult kidney
transplant patients which demonstrated similar outcomes.

Clinical Implications of Hypertonic
Fluid  Administration
An ideal alternative to conventional crystalloid and colloid fluids would be
inexpensive, produce minimal peripheral or pulmonary edema, generate
sustained hemodynamic effects, and be effective even if administered in
small volumes. Hypertonic, hypernatremic solutions, with or without added
colloid, appear to fulfill some of these criteria (Table 16-13).
Hypertonic solutions exert favorable effects on cerebral hemodynamics,
in part because of the reciprocal relationship between plasma osmolarity and
brain water.87 Resuscitation from hemorrhagic shock with lactated Ringer
solution has been shown to increase ICP, but resuscitation with 7.5% saline
did not.97 However, in a dog model with hemorrhagic shock and an
intracranial mass, hypertonic resuscitation demonstrated a delayed increase
in ICP.98 Despite concerns about hypertonicity and hypernatremia, acute
increases in serum sodium to 155 to 160 mEq/L produced no apparent harm
in hypovolemic trauma patients resuscitated with hypertonic saline (HS).99
The theoretical advantages of hypertonic fluids appear most attractive in
the acute resuscitation of hypovolemic patients who have decreased
intracranial compliance. Hypertonic solutions are also used to reduce brain
water and intracranial volume during neurosurgery and episodes of increased
ICP. Although reduced brain water can be accomplished with mannitol, some
clinicians prefer HS solutions.100 HS and mannitol of similar osmolarity
have similar effects on brain water, intracranial volume, and ICP. The most
salient difference between the two is that infusion of HS increases
intravascular volume, while diuresis secondary to mannitol decreases
intravascular volume. While few complications relate specifically to osmotic
therapy, acute severe hyperosmolarity could, theoretically, precipitate
blood–brain barrier opening. Clinical use of HS is associated, as is 0.9%
saline, with hyperchloremic acidosis, which usually requires no treatment,
but must be differentiated from other causes of metabolic acidosis. Although
there are plenty of theoretical benefits of HS in resuscitation of patients with
TBI, a randomized trial failed to demonstrate an improvement in outcome.101
The theoretical benefits of hypertonic fluid administration have also been
examined perioperatively. A 2016 Cochrane102 review including 18 studies
with 1087 adult perioperative patients ASA class I–III who received some

Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (HHS) and DKA represent two extremes
in the spectrum of decompensated DM. DKA is more common than HHS and
accounts for 1% of diabetes-related emergencies. The mortality rate for HHS
(10% to 20%) is greater than the mortality from DKA (5%).126 Patients may
present with a diabetic emergency, or the condition may develop
perioperatively because of stress, trauma, or infection.127
Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State
An occasional elderly patient with minimal or mild DM may present with
remarkably high blood glucose levels (>600 mg/dL) and profound
dehydration (9 to 12 L). It is often precipitated by concurrent illnesses such
as MI, stroke, sepsis, pneumonia, and other serious infections. Patients with
HHS usually have enough endogenous insulin activity to prevent lipolysis
and ketosis; even with blood sugar concentrations of 1,000 mg/dL, they are
not in ketoacidosis. It takes only one-tenth as much insulin to suppress
lipolysis as it does to stimulate glucose utilization. Presumably, it is the
combination of an impaired thirst response and decreased fluid intake in the
presence of relative insulin deficiency that allows the hyperglycemia to
develop. Hyperglycemia induces an osmotic diuresis that also contributes to
intravascular volume depletion. Marked hyperosmolarity may lead to coma
and seizures, and increased plasma viscosity predisposes patients to
intravascular thrombosis. HHS responds quickly to rehydration and small
doses of insulin. If there are no cardiovascular contraindications, 1 to 2 L (or
15 to 30 mL/kg) of normal saline should be infused over 1 hour. Insulin, by
bolus or infusion, should be administered after initial volume has been
administered.128 Administering insulin before adequate fluid replacement can
result in cardiovascular collapse. Insulin-mediated glucose uptake moves
water out of the intravascular space and into cells, causing severe
hypovolemia. With rapid correction of the hyperosmolarity, cerebral edema
is a risk, and recovery of mental acuity may be delayed after the blood
glucose level and circulating volume have been normalized.129 HHS tends to
have a substantially higher mortality than DKA, 15% versus 1% respectively,
in some clinical series.
Diabetic Ketoacidosis

results.rewritten_query: How does years age cerebellar infarction likewise result affect perioperative or critical-care decision making?

results.text:
cardiovascular disease.100–103 A particularly interesting risk factor, at least
for cardiac surgery patients, is an elevated arterial pulse pressure.104 This
finding supports the concept that age-related changes can influence
perioperative risk just as it appears to influence morbidity in the general
population. The original PeriOperative ISchemic Evaluation study (POISE)
raised the issue of perioperative β-blockade increasing the risk of stroke, but
when chronic, less intense β-blockade is used that risk is not present.105
Strokes typically occur well after surgery, on average 7 days later, although
upward of half occur within 24 hours.101,106 Most perioperative strokes are
ischemic and presumably from thromboembolism originating in the heart or
arteries. The contribution of hypotension to focal stroke is questionable,
although the POISE study could be cited as evidence that hypotension
increases stroke risk.101 Mortality after a perioperative stroke is
approximately twice that of a stroke in the general population, and overall
mortality from perioperative stroke is at least 20%.103,106
Table 34-5 Effect of Age on Selected Perioperative Complications
and Associated Mortalitya
As mentioned earlier, a recent significant change to nomenclature
classifying cognitive dysfunction in surgical patients has occurred.
Perioperative neurocognitive disorders (PND), which include delirium, are
subclassified based upon DSM-V criteria and temporal association with the
surgical procedure.36 This shift in nomenclature and diagnostic criteria is an
important step for clear identification, communication, and further study of
very common and impactful perioperative cognitive conditions in older

occurs in the perioperative period. Discussion of a patient’s goals and values
and consent to specific procedures can result in a “Limited Attempt at
Resuscitation” agreement between the anesthesiologist and patient.34
The prospect of functional impairment of physical capabilities may be
more worrisome to the older patient than even death. Elderly patients may
require a long time to return to their preoperative level of function, assuming
full recovery is even possible. For example, after major abdominal surgery,
most older patients require at least 3 months for ADLs and independent
ADLs to return to baseline.35 Persistent disability at 6 months varies with the
task. Although there is only a 9% incidence of persistent ADL deficits, there
is a 19% incidence of deficit in independent ADLs, and a 52% incidence of
diminished grip strength.
Functional impairment of neurocognition is common perioperatively. A
significant change to nomenclature classifying cognitive dysfunction in
surgical patients has occurred recently. To unify terminology in clinical and
research settings, “perioperative neurocognitive disorders” (PNDs) is now
used as the primary term for cognitive impairment identified in the pre or
postoperative periods (see Perioperative Complications for more details).36
Delirium remains a DSM-V diagnosis of acute onset change in mental
functions with confusion and decreased awareness of surroundings, occurring
within a week after surgery. This is an astoundingly common and costly
complication in older surgical patients. Pre-existing cognitive dysfunction,
also believed to be common (estimated in one study to be 25% of older
patients presenting to preop clinic for orthopedic surgery37), is an important
risk factor for postoperative complications, including delirium. Identification
of patients with baseline cognitive deficits is prudent for risk stratification,
but also has important implications for the informed consent process. Despite
this, execution of preoperative cognitive evaluation occurs “rarely” or
“never” for more than 80% of elderly patients.26 Discussion of postoperative
cognitive impairment is an important disclosure of risk when obtaining
informed consent,38 especially because of its high incidence, the gravity of
which seems lost on patients, despite a high degree of awareness. A recent
survey study of patients >70 years old preparing for surgery reported 83% of
patients were aware of postoperative delirium, with several patients (16%)
even reporting a previous personal experience with delirium, but concern
about the effects of delirium was low (92% of respondents had no more than

identification of sensitivity may lead to improved outcomes. Additional
studies show an association between intraoperative hypotension and other 1-
month adverse outcomes including cardiac events, kidney dysfunction, and
possibly stroke.94,95 Age above 68 was an independent risk factor in one of
these studies.96 Another study that looked at 1-year mortality found no
relation to intraoperative hypotension in the younger adult population, but
found that the elderly subjects had a higher 1-year mortality that
corresponded to the duration of the intraoperative hypotension.97 Again,
whether the hypotension is causal or just a marker is not clear, but these
studies do suggest that permitting prolonged hypotension may not be a good
idea.
Figure 34-13 Schematic outlining some factors associated with normal aging that put patients
at increased risk for adverse outcomes.
Because the mechanisms of aging contribute not only to normal aging but
also to the development and severity of disease, one might expect that age
and disease would interact in their contribution to perioperative risk.
Confirmation of such a hypothesis is provided by a prospective survey of
nearly 200,000 anesthetics in France.98 Both age and the number of chronic
diseases are associated with an increased rate of complications, but what is
particularly interesting is an apparent interaction between these two factors.
Figure 34-14 demonstrates that, for any given age group, the number of
complications increases with the number of comorbid diseases. To be young
and sick likely represents a special case as suggested by the point
representing the no more than 34-year-old group with three or more
comorbid diseases. Ignoring that outlier, connecting the dots of equal number
of comorbid diseases reveals a modest increase in risk with age for patients

Much remains to be studied to better understand what is now referred to
as postoperative neurocognitive disorder (NCD). Research using the newer
“mild” and “major” subclassification nomenclature is scarce, although the
new utilization of DSM-V criteria of both objective and subjective criteria
along with consideration for the impact on ADLs will most certainly result in
more meaningful and impactful research than the POCD literature has
produced to date. The question is whether anesthesia contributes to persistent
cognitive impairment well after surgery, and if so, to what degree? At this
time, understanding the POCD literature can still be valuable.
A seminal study not only examined surgical patients before and after
surgery but included control subjects who were tested over time as well, but
did not have surgery. At 3 months, a 10% incidence of POCD was observed
in the patients who had surgery, in comparison to a 3% incidence of similar
symptoms in those who did not.114 Risk factors for POCD at 3 months after
surgery include age, lower levels of education, prior history of stroke even in
the absence of residual deficit, and POCD at hospital discharge.115 Increased
mortality at 1 year is associated with patients who demonstrate cognitive
decline at both hospital discharge and at 3 months postoperatively.115 A study
that examined the association between exposure to surgery and anesthesia
and cognitive function in 8,503 pairs of middle-aged and elderly twins
identified 87 monozygotic and 124 dizygotic same-sexed twin pairs in whom
one had major surgery and the other did not. A statistically significant but
clinically negligible difference was found in neurocognitive testing. For
example, when participants were asked to provide as many names of animals
as possible in 1 minute, the mean number of animals named was 23.5 in those
that were exposed to surgery versus 24.2 in those that were not.116
Regarding the first few days after surgery, a few observations have been
made that are neither surprising nor particularly worrisome. First of all, there
is a similar degree of cognitive deficit in all age groups, not just the elderly.
Second, greater cognitive impairment can be observed with general
anesthesia than with regional anesthesia.117 Last, for the first few days, the
choice of general anesthesia makes a difference with propofol < desflurane <
sevoflurane in degree of cognitive decline. By 3 months, however, the
presence of POCD is almost exclusively a problem in older patients.118
Furthermore, no differences can be detected between general and regional
anesthetic options.119 By 1 year, even the best controlled studies suggest little

Figure 34-14 The interaction between age and comorbid disease. For each age bracket, as
comorbid disease increases, so does the rate of complications. The effect of age on the
complication rate is best visualized by examining points of equal comorbid disease. At zero
disease, only a modest increase in complications is observed with increasing age. At ever-
increasing degrees of comorbid disease, however, the increase in complications with age
becomes more and more pronounced. (Reproduced with permission from Tiret L, Desmonts
JM, Hatton F, Vourc’h G. Complications associated with anaesthesia–a prospective survey in
France. Can Anaesth Soc J. 1986;33:336–344. With kind permission from Springer
Science+Business Media.)
Central nervous system complications are also a major source of
morbidity and mortality. The most burdensome problems appear to be stroke,
postoperative delirium, and postoperative cognitive decline. All have the
potential to cause debilitating morbidity and an adverse impact on the
patient’s quality of life, their families, and the cost of medical care.
In a nonsurgical elderly population, there is an annual stroke incidence of
approximately 1%. The incidence of perioperative stroke in the older general
surgical population is approximately 0.5% (Table 34-5).99–101 Risk factors
include age, atrial fibrillation (odds ratio 2.0), history of a prior stroke or
TIA (odds ratio at least 1.6), recent myocardial infarction, COPD or current
smoking, acute renal dysfunction or chronic dialysis, the type of surgery, and
a variety of comorbid diseases including diabetes and renal or

Each of these changes and others can impact the practice of anesthesiology.116 Some important qualities and
skills, such as expertise, experience, and wisdom, are frequently enhanced. Others, such as creative thinking,
reaction time, and on-the-spot reasoning, may be compromised. These changes may be accompanied by other
physiologic changes that can exacerbate any coexisting sensory or cognitive impairment. The older
anesthesiologist who is experiencing these neurologic changes is potentially more prone to difficulties in
performing some of the critical processes necessary for safe anesthetic management, including rapid processing of
information, assimilation and application of new knowledge, complex decision making, and proper response
initiation.
There are also important changes in the cardiovascular and musculoskeletal systems that commonly occur and
can influence the practice of an aging anesthesiologist. Older anesthesiologists may lack adequate strength to
perform some of the more physically demanding aspects of practice. Night call can be especially challenging for
older physicians who tend to be more vulnerable to the adverse effects of sleep deprivation and may be more
susceptible to late-night errors.53
The relationship between age-related physiologic changes and professional competence is unclear. Older
physicians have been reported to experience an increased incidence of complications and disciplinary actions by
state licensing boards.117 And a meta-analysis that examined the relationship between years in practice and
markers of quality of care reported that 73% of the studies demonstrated decreasing performance with some or all
the outcomes assessed among physicians of advanced age.118 On the other hand, 22% of the studies reported no
association, or an improvement in performance with age.
The correlation between physicians’ age and actual clinical outcomes is even less clear because of wide
variability in research design including differences in procedure, procedure volume, and practice site. The
question of age-related competency has been studied extensively in the surgical specialties with various findings
of improved, unchanged, or worse outcomes when older surgeons are compared with younger colleagues.119
Neither has the relationship between advanced age and clinical outcomes among anesthesiologists been
definitively established. Suggestions of poorer outcomes come from studies that track surrogates such as the
frequency of complaints against senior anesthesiologists and the risk of being found responsible in professional
litigation.120 One study that employed high-frequency simulation to create a mock “cannot intubate/cannot
ventilate” scenario found an association between decreased proficiency in establishing an emergency
cricothyroidotomy and increased operator age and years from residency.121
Aging among anesthesiologists raises complex legal and ethical issues. Several federal laws are relevant to
the aging anesthesiologist’s rights and responsibilities regarding continuation of work. These include the Age
Discrimination Act, Title VII of the Civil Rights Act, the Medical and Family Leave Act, the Fair Labor Standards
Act, and the Employee Retirement Income Security Act. Both the American Medical Association and the
American College of Surgeons have issued statements that call for proactive steps to be taken toward monitoring
their aging members’ competency.115 However, both proposals rely on voluntary action by physicians, unlike many
other industries which impact public health, where advanced chronologic age automatically triggers limitations on
the range of professional activities. In many cases, the decision to limit or retire from practice remains at the sole
discretion of the individual anesthesiologist.
Age is one of the most important factors in decisions about retirement.122 The median age of retirement for
anesthesiologists as of 2012 was approximately 64 years.123 Commonly cited reasons for retirement among older
anesthesiologists include on-call responsibilities, financial considerations, lack of professional satisfaction,
health concerns, and changes in governmental policies and the health care operating environment. The decision to
retire from anesthesiology is frequently precipitated by concerns about deteriorating clinical skills. In many

the concurrently growing interest in broader initiatives aimed at improving
comprehensive geriatric surgical care, with multidisciplinary and
multicomponent approaches to better preparing and managing older surgical
patients, will ultimately result in focused and tailored function-preserving or
even function-improving postoperative outcomes in our geriatric patients.
In summary, there are a few specific points about the conduct of
anesthesia that can be made. Besides the obvious caveats (“avoid
hypotension and hypoxia”), the basic approach to an anesthetic for an elderly
patient can be described as cautious. Since stroke is likely a thromboembolic
phenomenon, there may be little that can be done beyond standard, good
anesthetic care. However, it is not clear that antiplatelet therapy needs to be
discontinued for surgery as much as currently occurs. The efficacy of statins
has also not been adequately assessed. As previously discussed, drug
choices and dosage have a potentially major impact on delirium. Pain control
with multimodal therapy to reduce opioid consumption is probably a good
thing, but poor pain control may be as bad as too much opioid. Finally, it is
not clear what the relationship is between anesthesia and cognitive decline,
if there is one at all. Given that unsatisfactory statement, it seems reasonable
to choose the anesthetic technique based on the other factors germane to the
patient and surgery.
The Future
I will never be an old man. To me, old age is always 15 years older than I
am.
—Francis Bacon
Improvements in surgical and anesthetic techniques that reduce the overall
stress to the patient are permitting more surgeries to be performed on older
and sicker patients than ever before. Nevertheless, the older patient will
continue to experience the majority of perioperative adverse outcomes. Much
remains to be accomplished in the quest to find ways to decrease the
incidence and severity of those adverse outcomes. The most pressing issues
are arguably the prevention of postoperative delirium, cognitive decline,
pneumonia, respiratory failure, and cardiovascular complications. Improved

cerebral artery. It is unclear whether application of such monitoring
decreases the incidence of perioperative neurologic complications.
The etiology of perioperative neurologic complications is believed to be
ischemic injury, either related to emboli (air, atheroma, other particulate
matter) or cerebral hypoperfusion in susceptible patients (e.g., pre-existing
cerebrovascular disease). Ischemic strokes vastly outnumber hemorrhagic
strokes in the perioperative setting, tend to be focal and most likely are a
consequence of macroembolization. Cognitive dysfunction probably results
from microembolization, hypoperfusion, inflammation, and/or silent
infarctions. Risk factors for neurologic complications include advanced age
(>70 years), pre-existing cerebrovascular disease (e.g., carotid artery
stenosis >80%), history of prior stroke, peripheral vascular disease,
ascending aortic atheroma, and diabetes. Surgical risk factors that influence
neurologic outcomes include the duration of CPB, intracardiac procedure
(e.g., valve replacement), aortic pressure, excessive warming during and
following 
CPB, 
and 
perioperative 
hypotension.73 
Intraoperative
hyperglycemia, hypoglycemia, and postoperative glycemic variability have
been associated with neurocognitive injury.74–76
Embolization during cardiac surgery is related to aortic atheroma burden;
patients with mobile atheromas are most susceptible. Suggested surgical
strategies for patients with severely diseased aortas who are at increased
risk of stroke, include performing surgery under hypothermic fibrillatory
arrest with LV venting and avoidance of aortic cross-clamping, applying a
single cross-clamp (i.e., distal and proximal grafts performed during same
cross-clamp), relocation of proximal grafts to an area of nondiseased aorta,
no proximal grafts (internal mammary arteries only), using off-pump
coronary artery bypass, or hypothermic circulatory arrest with resection and
graft replacement of the diseased aortic segment.
Cerebral oximetry, which is based on the Beer-Lambert law and
principles of optical spectrometry, has gained widespread clinical
acceptance due to ease of application, interpretation of monitoring data, and
validated protocols to treat desaturations. Numerical values of regional
cerebral saturation (rScCO2) range from 60% to 80%. It is important to get
bilateral baseline values, prior to administration of sedative medications and
oxygen. An absolute decrease of rScCO2 value to ≤50% or a decrement from

results.query: Give a structured review of occur antepartum peripartum care regional prenatal with practical bedside implications.
results.rewritten_query: Give a structured review of occur antepartum peripartum care regional prenatal with practical bedside implications.

results.text:
occur. Antepartum and peripartum care in a regional prenatal health center
(Maternal Level of Care IV) is recommended.
The mode of delivery for patients with PAH is controversial. Vaginal
delivery is associated with smaller hemodynamic shifts and decreased risk
of bleeding. However, emergency cesarean delivery for maternal or fetal
deterioration may be required. Planned cesarean delivery offers the
advantage of ensuring optimal conditions and availability of experienced
staff.
Pain during labor and vaginal delivery is especially detrimental because
it may further increase pulmonary vascular resistance and decrease CO.
Neuraxial analgesia is useful in attenuating these hemodynamic changes.
General and epidural anesthesia have been used successfully for cesarean
delivery; however, single-shot spinal anesthesia is not recommended due to
the abrupt decrease in SVR associated with this technique. Maternal
mortality from PAH is estimated between 16% and 30%, with most deaths
due to right heart failure during labor or the early postpartum period.112
Cardiomyopathy
Peripartum cardiomyopathy is left ventricular systolic dysfunction occurring
late in pregnancy or in the first 6 weeks postpartum. Most cases are
diagnosed after delivery. It occurs in approximately 1 in 3,000 births and is
associated with a maternal mortality of 10% to 25%.112 Peripartum
cardiomyopathy is a diagnosis of exclusion; the cause is thought to be related
to myocarditis or an abnormal immune response. Risk factors include AMA,
multiparity, 
multiple 
gestation, 
smoking, 
diabetes, 
obesity, 
and
preeclampsia.112 Good long-term prognosis is related to recovery of left
ventricular function within 6 months of delivery. Left ventricular ejection
fraction of less than 30% at diagnosis, significant left ventricular dilation,
and involvement of the right ventricle are associated with adverse outcomes,
and these patients should be counseled against future pregnancies. Medical
management includes preload optimization, afterload reduction, and therapy
to 
improve 
myocardial 
contractility. 
Some 
patients 
require
thromboprophylaxis or automatic implantable cardioverter defibrillator
placement. Intrapartum anesthetic management goals include avoiding
